match.abstractText: Human genetic studies of critical COVID-19 pneumonia have revealed the essential role of type I interferon-dependent innate immunity to SARS-CoV-2 infection. Conversely, an association between the HLA-B*15:01 allele and asymptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection in unvaccinated individuals was recently reported, suggesting a contribution of pre-existing T cell-dependent adaptive immunity. We report a lack of association of classical HLA alleles, including HLA-B*15:01, with pre-omicron asymptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection in unvaccinated participants in a prospective population-based study in the US (191 asymptomatic vs. 945 symptomatic COVID-19 cases). Moreover, we found no such association in the international COVID Human Genetic Effort cohort (206 asymptomatic vs. 574 mild or moderate COVID-19 cases and 1,625 severe or critical COVID-19 cases). Finally, in the Human Challenge Characterisation study, the three HLA-B*15:01 individuals infected with SARS-CoV-2 developed symptoms. As with other acute primary infections, no classical HLA alleles favoring an asymptomatic course of SARS-CoV-2 infection were identified. These findings suggest that memory T-cell immunity to seasonal coronaviruses does not strongly influence the outcome of SARS-CoV-2 infection in unvaccinated individuals.

citations.title: Silent battles: immune responses in asymptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection
Gene: environment interactions in immune and inflammatory responses to severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 infection

narrative:
Short impact summary

The scholarly article titled "Lack of association between HLA and asymptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection" contributes important insights into the intersection of immunology and infectious disease research. Published on medRxiv and backed by key institutions such as the Centre International de Recherche en Infectiologie and the Garvan Institute of Medical Research, the work underscores the complexities of understanding asymptomatic infections amid the COVID-19 pandemic.

Full narrative

The research published in 2023 provides an investigation into the relationship between human leukocyte antigens (HLA) and asymptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infections. This study is significant as it emerges from prestigious institutions including the École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne and Pitié-Salpêtrière Hospital. The backing of the Swiss National Science Foundation further highlights the importance of this research in contributing to our understanding of the immune response to COVID-19.

Despite the current citation count of 2 in notable journals like *Cellular and Molecular Immunology* and *Current Opinion in Immunology*, the work emphasizes foundational aspects of virus-host interactions. The absence of community engagement metrics, such as those on platforms like Reddit or Wikipedia, suggests that while the research has scholarly merit, it may not yet have entered broader public discourse.

The findings of this study may inform future immunological research and the development of therapies targeted at combating SARS-CoV-2. By not identifying a significant correlation between HLA genes and asymptomatic infection, this study invites further inquiry into genetic predispositions and resilience to viral infections, a topic of critical importance in current biomedical research.

In addition to the implications of the results, the research signifies a wider academic impact, being recognized in subsequent works that demonstrate the foundational nature of these findings. This downstream reuse of the data reflects a growing interest in leveraging such studies to inform understanding within the field of immunology, marking an important step in addressing the effects and challenges posed by the COVID-19 pandemic.
